title: Kolrosing: incised line decoration
subtitle: The Scandinavian technique of incised lines filled with dark pigment on carved wood
excerpt: Kolrosing is a traditional Scandinavian surface decoration: fine lines incised into a carved wooden surface with a knife, then rubbed with dark pigment; traditionally ground charcoal, coffee, or dark soil; to make the lines visible as permanent dark marks. The technique requires no specialist equipment and works on any smooth-grained hardwood piece.
sourceNotes: Traditional Norwegian and Swedish kolrosing practice; Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) for incised-line wood decoration and pigment-filling methods.

glossaryTerms.definition: A Scandinavian surface decoration technique: fine lines incised into wood with a knife tip, filled with dark pigment, and sealed with oil to produce permanent dark marks.
Cutting a fine line into a surface; shallower and narrower than a chip-carved groove; using just the knife tip under hand pressure alone.
The fill material that colours the incised lines in kolrosing; traditionally ground charcoal, dark soil, or dried cinnamon or coffee grounds.

content.text: is one of the simplest forms of surface decoration in wood: a marking knife tip is pulled along the design lines under hand pressure alone, cutting a fine groove into the wood surface. A dark

is rubbed into the grooves, the excess wiped off, and an oil finish applied over the top. The lines emerge as permanent dark marks in the finished surface. The technique requires no specialist equipment; any marking knife and a piece of charcoal will produce the result; and can be applied to any finished carved or whittled piece with a smooth, close-grained surface.

Traditional kolrosing designs are geometric: crossed lines, chevrons, diamond grids, and stylised plant forms built from simple repeated line elements. Draw the design in pencil on the wood surface before

. The pencil lines are guides only; the incised line does not need to follow them exactly, and a slight wavering in the line is part of the character of hand-incised work. The design should be composed of straight lines and gentle curves; tight curves require the knife tip to be lifted and repositioned, which can produce visible join points in the line.

Close-grained, pale hardwoods produce the clearest kolrosing: sycamore, birch, cherry, and maple all work well. The wood must be smooth and fully seasoned; rough-sawn or partially seasoned wood tears rather than takes a clean incised line. Birch and sycamore are the best starting materials: their fine, even grain does not redirect the knife tip. Avoid oak and ash; the pronounced ring-porous grain redirects the tip along the softer earlywood rather than following the intended line.

Pull the marking knife tip along the design line under hand pressure alone; do not use a mallet or drive the blade with the forearm. The incised line should be 0.5 to 1 mm wide and approximately 0.5 mm deep. Test the depth on the back of the piece: the groove should be visible but not feel like a step under the fingernail. Too shallow and the pigment will not stay in the groove after rubbing; too deep and the line reads as a chip-carved groove rather than a kolrosing mark. Work all straight lines with a straight edge; curves freehand. Keep the knife sharp throughout; a dull tip tears the grain at the sides of the groove.

Traditional kolrosing pigments are all natural: ground charcoal (the most common), dark soil, dried and ground coffee grounds, or ground cinnamon for a warm red-brown. Grind the pigment fine; a mortar and pestle or the back of a spoon on a flat stone. Rub the powder into the incised lines with a finger, working it across the whole design area until all grooves are full. Wipe the surface with a dry cloth, removing the pigment from the flat faces while leaving it in the grooves. The grooves hold the powder; the flat faces do not. If pigment remains on the flat faces, wipe more firmly; do not use a damp cloth, which smears the powder into the grain.

Apply a thin coat of raw linseed oil or walnut oil over the entire surface immediately after wiping the excess pigment. The oil seals the pigment into the grooves permanently and prevents it from washing out with normal use. Do not use boiled linseed oil or Danish oil at this stage; both contain drying agents that can react with natural charcoal pigment. Apply the first oil coat thin; one wipe with an oiled cloth. For a spoon or bowl, let this oil coat set fully (3 to 4 days for raw linseed oil, 2 to 3 days for walnut oil) before using the piece with food.

The traditional Norwegian kolrosing design vocabulary uses: the running border (a continuous line of chevrons or diamonds around the rim of a piece), the star or compass rose at the centre or base, and the stylised flower or plant form as a central motif. These three elements combine into almost every traditional piece. For a first kolrosing project, a simple running diamond border around the handle of a spoon is the conventional starting point: one horizontal line and diagonal lines at 60 degrees crossing it, producing a grid of diamonds along the handle length.
